CITY OF SAN RAFAEL 
SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA 
f INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUMl 
DATE: March 6, 1981 
TO: 
Jack G. Nixon, Chairman, San Rafael Redevelopment Agency 
FROM 
Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney 
SUBJECT Conflict of Interest Concerning the 
Third Street Project Area 
I ~m in receipt of a copy of a letter to Mr. Beyer from 
the Fair Political Practices Commission, Attorney, Barbara 
Milman. 
Ms. Milman states that Mr. Rosenthal has correctly 
pointed out the law in the area. 
However, Ms. Millman goes 
on to say, "it's not always easy to determine in the 
specific factual situation whether disqua1ifi8ation is 
necessary". 
Then Ms. Millman goes on to say, as I under-
stand it, no advice is expressly being requested on this 
set of facts. 
Based on that situation, I would respectfully suggest this 
matter be put over and the facts be submitted to the F.P.P.C. 
for evaluation and decision. 
The facts should be as 
suggested in my February 2, 1981 memo to you. 
It is my opinion that the factual situation with respect 
to the project area and its impact on McPhail's is fraught 
with the potential for a legal conflict of interest. 
Prudence would dictate that this matter be truly investi-
gated and determined before action is taken.

cc: F.P.P.C., Ms. Barbara Milman 


CITY OF SAN RAFAEL 
SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA 
I 'NTf!R-DEPARTMENTAL MEMOMANOUM 1 
February 2, 1981 
TO: 
Jack G. Nixon, Councilmember 
FROM: 
Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney 
SUBJECT: 
Conflict of Interest Concerning the 
Third Street Project Area 
You reques ted my corrunen ts on the opinion da ted January 13, 
1980 (sic), prepared by Mr. Rosenthal (copy attached) 
regarding a possible conflict of interest concerning yourself 
with respect to voting on a Negative Declaration fQr the 
Third Street improvement project. 
My comments are as follows: 
McPhail's: 
Mr. Rosenthal's analysis lacks an adeqriate factual foundation 
to support his conclusion. 
Whether the proposed decision will have a "material financial 
effect" on McPhail's can be shown if the decision will 
increase or decrease the gross revenue of McPhail's by the 
lesser of $100,000 or 1%. 
If net revenues are calculated a 
"material financial effect" on McPhail's can be shown if the 
decision will increase or decrease the net revenues by the 
lesser of $50,000 or 1/2% (2 Cal. Ad. Code §18702). 
Mr. Rosenthal's analysis makes no effort to try to find out 
what, if any, change in McPhail's revenue resulted from the 
removal of parking on Third Street and the addition of a 
parking lot across the street from McPhail's. 
In view of the fact that this decision has already been 
made, certainly McPhail's has the necessary revenue informa-
K 
tion which they may wish to make available. 
In the absence 
of direct information from McPhail's, the question of making 
assumptions concerning McPhail's should be given to the 
Planning Department who can estimate, based on the varied 
statistical information available to them, whether or not 
the McPhail's business had the requisite increase or decrease 
in gross or net income. 


OTHER POSSIBLE CONFLICTS 
With respect to other possible conflicts in the project 
area, the opinion gives no advice. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission, in the January 15, 
1981 letter to the undersigned, states: 
"In summary, if Mr. Nixon's pro rata share of gross receipts 
paid by a client or customer to a business entity of which 
he and his spouse have a 10% or greater interest is between 
$250 and $10,000, Mr. Nixon is required to disqualify 
himself from any decision which will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on that source of income, but until he determines, 
with or without the advice of counsel, that disqualification 
is required, he is not obliged to disclose that client or 
customer." 
Obviously, the key to the requirement to disclose is whether 
a governmental decision by you will have a material financial 
effect distinguishable from its effect in the public generally 
in business entities located in the project area. 
Two Fair Political Practices Commission cases are on point: 
2 Fair Political Practices Commission 77 (June 2; 1976.) 
City of Davis 23 square block "core area" 
A Planning Commissioner owns a home across the street from 
the boundary of the core area; he can vote. 
Another Planning Commissioner owns a vacant lot in the core 
area; he cannot vote. 
A Councilmember is a limited partner in a retail store 
within the cor.e areai he can vote. 
4 Fair Political Practices Commission 19 (Februar.y 7, 1978.) 
City of San Clemente 6 to 7 square block "improvement area" 
One Councilmember owns his own law office and another owns a 
locksmith business in the improvement district and it is 
ruled that neither can vote. 
The size of the district and its relation to the rest of the 
town appears to be the key to whether or not a governmental 
decision for a downtown business area will have a material 
financial effect distinguishable from the public generally. 
In the San Clemente case at page 3, the Fair Political 
Practices Commission states: 


"In this case, the relevant category of downtown commercial 
property owners is a small one relative to either the class 
of all San Clemente commercial property owners or the entire 
San Clemente business community. 
Approximately 50% of the 
commercial property in the city is located outside the 
proposed assessment district. 
As in the case of the Davis 
commercial lessors, we do not believe that the class of 
downtown commercial property owners can be considered a 
significant segment of the public. 
Because the improvement 
project is limited to the downtown area, commercial property 
in the improvement district will reap direct benefits and 
incur district costs that will not be shared by other 
commercial property in the city. 
In fact, increased downtown 
business and the concomitant increase in downtown property 
values may be gained at the expense of commercial property 
in other parts of the city. 
Therefore, the effect of the 
p~oposed decisions will be distinguishable from their effect 
on the public generally. 
Consequently, the two councilmembers 
must disqualify themselves from decisions concerning creation 
of the improvement district." 
It appears to me that the small size of the San Rafael 
project area and the nature of the improvements being made 
make the business entities in the project area distinguishable 
from the public generally. 
Pursuant to the foregoing, I conclude that if you have income 
of $250 or more from a business entity within th~ project area 
and a governmental decision you make will have a significant 
financial effect on such entity as detailed above you must not 
vote and you must also disclose the disqualifying financial 
interest. 
The Fair Political Practices· Commission, in their 
letter of January 15, 1981, states: 
" .•. if Mr. Nixon decides that he has a conflict of interest 
in a particular decision within the meaning of the Political 
Reform Act, his determination not to participate in that 
decision must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial 
interest, made part of the Redevelopment Agency's official 
record. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(b) (5). 
If he has a 
conflict because the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on a source of income of less than $10,000 to his 
business entity, then this disclosure at the time of disquali-
fication will result in the disclosure of the name of a 
client or customer who was not required to be reported on 
his statement of economic interests." 
If you have any questions, please call me.

Dear Mr. Beyer: 
RECErVED 
JAN 1 3 1981 
CITY ATTORNEY 
CITY OF SAN RAFAEL 
At the request of the Agency, I am ~riting with regard to 
the alleged conflict of interest of Agency member Jack Nixon 
in the matter of the negiltive declaration on the 3rd Street 
Improvement project. 
The facts as I ullc1erst,:ll1d them are as follows: 
Hr. Nixon 
is an insurance salesman. 
In his insurance business, Mr. Nixon 
has received income of more than $250 in the past year from 
commissions on the sale of insurance policies to 11cPhails. 
McPhails has a retail business that is located at the corner of 
3rd and C Streets. 
The entrance of McPhails is on C Street, and 
it has adequate off-street parking at the rear of its building. 
The decision about which the conflict of interest question 
arises involves issuance of a negative declaration under the 
California Environmental Quality Act with respect to the 3rd Street 
Improvement project. 
This project involves elimination of about 
50 parking spaces on 3rd Street and making certain improvements in 
the traffic flow on that street. 
Of the 50 spaces eliminated, . 
approximately 15 are within a block of McPhails. 
The project was 
proposed and approved by the Agency in 1978 allegedly without com-
pliance with The Califo niL! Environmental Quality Act (IfCEQAtI). 
Owners of business s on 3rd Street then brought suit against the 
Agency seeking to overtu n the approval on grounds that CEQA was 
not complied in approviny the project. 
At the request of the owners, 
the Superior Court issued il preliminary injunction preventing imple-
mentation of the 
oject. 
The Agency then certified a negative 
declaration for the proj 
t, and th 
court thereafter dissolved the 
preliminary injunction. 
After 
e injunction was dissolved, the Agency 
went forward with and 
leted the project in 1979. 
Trial of the owners' 
uit was held in 1980, and the trial court 
RECEIVEr 
III •• 
~ _ 
' .. 


.. ~ 
Robert Beyer 
January 12, 1980 
Page 2 
judge ordered the Agency to make a new determination as to whether 
or not a negative declaration for the project should be issued. 
In its order the court specifically ordered the Agency to consider 
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street in light of other projects 
that have been undertaken to provide off-street parking in the 
vicinity of the 3rd Street area. 
It is this new negative declaration 
determination that raises the conflict of interest question for 
Mr. Nixon. 
Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, an official cannot 
participate in a decision if it is "reasonably forseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally," on a source of income 
from whom the official has received $250 in income in the previous 
year. 
Government Code Section 87103. 
Under the peculiar circumstances of this case, it is not reasonably 
forseeable that the decision with regard to the negatfve declaration 
will have a material financial effect upon Mr. Nixon's source of 
income, McPhails. 
To begin the decision before the "Agency is not the 
substantive decision as to whether or not to do the project, but 
rather a procedural step, i.e. whether or not to prepare an environ-
mental impact report. 
That decision standing alone will have no 
financial effect on McPhails. 
In normal circumstances, the decision 
whether or not to prepare an environmental impact report is made as 
a preliminary step in the process of determining whether to undertake 
a project. 
In those circumstances the decision concerning preparation 
of the impact report conceivably could have a material financial 
effect if it is reasonably forseeable that the report will impact the 
substantive decision regarding the project itself. 
In this instance, however, the decision regarding the project 
has already been made, and the project itself has been carried out. 
Therefore, it is highly unlikely that an environmental impact report 
will affect the SUbstantive decision to carry out the project. 
See 
idents Ad Hoc Stadium Comm. v. Board of Trustees, 89 Ca.App.3d 274 
Therefore, it cannot be said that it is reasonably forseeable 
that the decision regarding the negative declaration will affect the 
decision with regard to the project in such a manner so as to have 
a material financial effect on McPhails. 
Even if we were 
ling here with the substantive decision to 
proceed with the proje 
, it would be difficult to conclude that the 
decision would have a material financi~l effect upon McPhails. 
The 
project eliminated parking on 3rd Street and somewhat altered the 
traffic flow on that ~)trcet. 
It is not reasonably forseeable that 


Rohert Beyer 
January 13, 1980 
Page 3 
removal of spaces will mate:r:ially .:1ffect tvlcPhails. 
The business is 
not oriented to 3rd street but rather to C Street, and it has its 
own off-street parking. 
Thus, it is not forseeable that the elimin-
ation of the few sp.:1ces on 3rd Street in the immediate vicinity of 
McPhails would m.:1terially affect the volume of customers going to 
McPhails. 
Therefore, the income of the business would not be 
materially affected. 
It should be noted that regUlations of the Fair political Practices 
Commission define "material fin<:lllCi.:11 effect" as an effect of S100,000 
or 1% per year on gross income or $50,000 or 1/2% per year on net 
income. 
2.Cal.Adm. Code Section 18703. 
It is highly unlikely that 
the elimination of 
rking on 3rd Street will have the magnitude of 
effect on McPhails to be considered material given the fact that 
McPhails has sales of five to ten million dollars or more a year. 
The other aspect of the project -
improvement of traffic flow 
in the downtown area of San Rafael -
may affect McPhails in that 
circulation in the area will be improved. 
However, all businesses 
in the downtown area will be affected in substantially the same manner 
by improved circulation. 
The segment of the populatioh consisting 
of all downtown business~s is large enough to be considered the 
"public generally" for purposes of determining whether a material 
financial effect on a business is distinguishable from the effect 
on the public generally. 
In opinion Requested by William L. Owen, 
City Attorney, City of Davis, 2 FPPC Ops.77 (1976). 
Thus, even if 
the traffic aspect of tile decision .:1ffects 'McPhails m.:1terially, Mr. 
Nixon need not disqualify himself since the deci~ion affects all 
downtown b~sinesses in the same m.:1nner. 
With respect to Mr. Duggan's letter of January 5, 1980, to 
Mr. Nixon, a comment is uppropriate. 
Contrary to Mr. Duggan's 
letter, the California Supreme Court, in Huyes v. Wood, 25 Cal.3d 
772 (1979), struck down Government Code Section 87207 (b) (2) as 
violative of equal protection and held that the threshold for dis-
closure of income by br 
ers ~nd attorneys should be that provided 
in Government Code Section 87207 (b) (3) for other professions, i.e., 
$10,000. 
Thus, contrary to the statements in Nr. Duggan's letter, 
Mr. Nixon's threshold for disclosure of a source of income from 
his insurance business on a statement of economic interest is 
$10,000 in a year, not $1,000 in a year. 
In his letter, Mr. Dug 
n also notes the construction of parking 
lots across from McPhails uS a possible source of a conflict of 
interest. 
As I understand it, the construction of those lots was 
not part of the project for which the original negative declaration 
was issued. 
The effect of those lots obviously relates to the 
environmental 
ct of removal of 
rking irt that the impact of 
removal is mitigated by availability of alternative parking. 
This 


. ,. 
I. '~o' 
$; 
Robert Beyer 
January 13, 1980 
Page 4 
issue is discussed in both the 1978 and 1980 initial studies for the 
negative declaration. 
lIm-lever, I do not see the 1980 negative 
declaration decision as being one prepared for the decision to build 
that off-street parking. 
Furthermore, even if the "project" for 
which the 1980 negative declaration is prepared is viewed as both 
th~ removal of parking from 3rd Street and the off-street parking, 
it is not forseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on McPhails. 
Because the Agency has already purchased the land 
for and constructed the lots, it is highly unlikely that a decision 
regarding the negative declaration will have any impact on a decision 
that has already been made and implemented at a large cost to the 
Agency. 
Sincerely I

Re: 
Request for Confirmation of Correctness of Advice Given 
to San Rafael Redevelopment Agency Chairman Jack Nixon 
Dear Attorney Cameron, 
Please find enclosed a memorandum prepared by me to Jack Nixon, 
Chairman of the San Rafael Redevelopment Agency. 
It is my 
position that before Mr. Nixon can vote on the matter at hand 
he must reveal,for factual and legal evaluation, income ex-
ceeding $250 from persons or businesses within the project 
area. 
The City Council has requested an opinion from special 
Redevelopment Agency counsel on this matter. 
I have enclosed 
their request as well for your inspection. 
The next meeting of the Agency is on January 19, 1981. 
I 
would appreciate a letter from you confirming the correctness 
of the memorandum I provided to Mr. Nixon or, in the alterna-
tive, any comments you might have with respect to any error 
or omission in my memorandum. 
If you have any questions, please call me. 
Sincerely,

I 'NTER-OEPARTMENTAL M=EMORANO~M 1 
HAND 
DELIVERED 
DATEI 
Januar.y 5 I' 1980 
TO: 
Jack Nixon, Chairman, San Rafael Redevelopment Agency 
FROM: 
Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney 
SUBJECT: 
Possible Conflict of Interest in 
Third Street Project Area 
I am in receipt of a letter from attorneys Bernstein and 
Peterson alleging that you have an unspecified conflict of 
interest-with respect to the Third Street project area. 
I understand you were contacted by Mr. Bullard and were not 
able to offer any comment as to what conflict Messers. 
Bernstein and Peterson are referring to. 
I have reviewed your latest Fair Political Practices Commis-
sion Form 721 for January 1, 1979 to December 31, 1979, in 
which you declare having income in excess of $1,000 from 
MCPhail's which has a major outlet at Third and PC" Streets 
which is within the Third Street project area. 
Whether this or any other interest you might have in the 
project area disqualifies you from voting is a mixed question 
of fact and law which should be analyzed by City Planner.s 
and the office of the City Attorney before a vote is taken. 
Should you vote without the necessary analysis, the action 
taken will be subject to court challenge and may result in 
the action of the Redevelopment Agency being set aside or 
being declared void pursuant to §91003(b) of the Government 
Code. 
You may, of course, avoid the problem by abstaining from any 
further participation or vote in the matter. 
ANALYSIS 
You are required to disclose income annually pursuant to 
§87207(b) of the Government Code which provides in pertinent 
part: 
" 
(2) In the case of a business entity 
which provides legal or brokerage services, 
the name of every person who paid fees to 
the business entity if the filer's pro rata 
share of fees from such person was equal to 
or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000)." 


Chairman Jack Ni 
January 5, 1980 
Page Two 
This section was upheld in Hays v. Wood 25 C 3d 772, 160 
Cal. Rptr. 102, and a procedure was established to withhold 
the identity of a source of income. 
In any case, an income 
statement for 1980 is not yet due (see Government Code 
§87203) and it is unknown if you had the same income from 
MCPhail's in 1980 as you did in 1979. 
You are required to 
dis9ualify yourself from voting where a governmental 
dec1sion is to be made in which you have a financial interest 
(Government Code §87100). 
A financial interest is present "if it is reasonably foresee-
able that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect-on the public generally on 
••• any source of income ••• aggregating $250.00 or more in 
value ••• to the public official ••• " (Government Code §87103). 
This mea~s that if you have clients in the Third Street 
project area which you have not previously disclosed because 
the income to you was less than $1,000, you must now disclose 
any such clients who have paid you as little as $250 in the 
last 12 months. 
Such income may prove, upon analysis, to 
disquulify you from voting. 
The more than $1,000 income 
from McPhail's, if again received in 1980, appears to 
disqualify you from voting on this matter for three reasons: 
1. It exceeds $250. 
2. 
MCPhail's is directly impacted by the plan due to its 
location at Third and nCR Streets. 
3. 
Substantial enhancement to the McPhail property by the 
addition by the Redevelopment Agency of a parking lot 
lot next to the McPhail store. 
The Planning Department analysis of the financial effect will 
allow legal guidelines to be applied which will provide a 
basis to judge whether or not a material financial effect on 
your income is foreseeable.

Agency Secretary

Lee C. Rosenthal, Esq.

1333 Broadway, Suite 825 
Oakland, California 
94612 
January 6 8 198. 
Re: 
Legal Opinion Regarding Agency Chairman Jack Nixon -
Third Street Improvement Project -
Negative Declaration 
Dear Lee: 
Following the public hearing regarding the captioned matter, the Redevelopment 
Agency informally decided to postpone taking formal action pending the outcome 
of a legal opinion to be drafted which addresses the ability of Redevelopment 
Agency Chairman Jack Nixon to participate in the referenced matter, taking 
into consideration the fact that McPhail's (which has a retail outlet bor-
dering on Third Street) is a source of income for Mr. Nixon. 
Although, strictly speaking, Mr. Nixon's true sources of income are the 
principal agents, i.e., the large insurance companies, he has nonetheless 
listed those customers or clients from whom he receives more than a $1,000 
annually in income. 
McPhail's is one such source of income. 
The retail store 
in San Rafael on Third and C Streets is but one part of the entity which com-
prises McPhail's, a Marin County building supplier. 
(See enclosed description 
from 1 FPPC Opinions 199, December 4, 1975.) 
Because of a prior opinion rendered by your office in this matter (see enclosec 
letter) we feel it appropriate for you again to render an opinion concerning 
this complex and difficult area of law. 
If you require further information as a factual basis for your opinion, do not 
hesitate to contact either myself or Mr. Nixon at 472-0232. 
Thank you for your assistance.

the MMWD.

except ready-mix concrete, where it has only three major compe-

! am writing this in response to your telephone request this afternoon 
concerning the question of conflict of interest as it relates to Agency 
conceptual action on the ROMA atudy. 
As I understand from you, the specific 
concern raised here 1s a question as to whether or not a council member 
whose business is that of an insurance broker would be precluded from voting 
in matters relating to land use study presently being prepared by ROMA inasmuch 
as the study deals with land utilization in Downtown San Rafael which private 
property may be insured by a member of the Council. 
As I am sure you are aware, a determination as to what is a conflict 
under Proposition Nine is an extremely difficult one to make and to date the 
Commission itself has failed to promulgate determinative regulations in 
regard to the conflict issue. 
As such, each decision must be made on a caae 
by case basis. 
If one were to take Proposition Nine to its ultimate absurdity, 
it is clear that the fact that one insures property within the City could 
create a conflict under Proposition Nine. 
The result of this would be that 
no person could serve on the council who generally did business within the 
City, for clearly every decision will have some effect on one's business. 
The determining factor here is whether the decision would have a material 
financial effect. 
Without knowing the specifics of the properties insured 
by any member of the council and given the fact that the ROMA study is just 
that - a study - it would be my opinion that upon disclosure of the business 
interests involved, the Council person could participate in decisions relating 
to the conceptual ROMA plan and the land use provisions contained within it. 


Mr. William Biels~r 
Page Two 
3/5/76 
'-
It would not be permissible to vote on a matter which specifically related 
to a client's property other than in a general nature as it affects the 
land use patterns within the Downtown S8 8 whole. 
For example. if at some 
time 1n order to implement the policies of the Agency 88 it affected the 
Central San Rafael Redevelopment Project Area, decisions had to be made 8S 
to whether or not to acquire specific property, if the Councilperson insured 
the building which was under consideration for acquisition. it might well be 
a conflict depending on the specific facts of that situation to participate 
in the decision to acquire. 
As such it ,,,auld be my opinion that the Council 
person must refrain from participating in such a decision. 
However, I 
believe it is permissible for this Councilperson, based on the facts I have 
available to.me at this time to participate in the decision-making as it 
relates to the land use proposals in the ROMA study provided a complete 
disclosure is made at the public meeting at which such participation and 
decision-making is to take place as to the types of business relationships 
that Councilperaon has within the project area relative to his insurance 
business. 
I realize that I have not provided you with a clear-cut opinion on 
conflict of interest at this time. 
This is partially due to the limited 
facts I presently have available to me and even more importantly because 
of the lack of guidance presently available from the Fair Political Practices 
Commission in regard to conflict of interest. 
As this matter will become 
increasingly important, it would be my suggestion that we get together to 
develop specific guidelines for Agency use as it relates to conflict of interest.

Yours very trul},:.'-' "._-,.'"

sections.question: Whether the Fair Political Practices Commission intends to intervene in litigation between the requester and Mr. Smith, and specific questions regarding disqualification requirements and conflict of interest determinations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: The Commission does not intend to intervene in the litigation. Section 91003(a) does not mandate that a plaintiff file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunctive relief; such administrative exhaustion is required only when seeking damages or penalties under sections 91004, 91005, 91007. The Political Reform Act requires disqualification only in decisions where a public official has a financial interest (Section 87100), but does not provide for removal from office. The FPPC cannot provide advice on specific factual situations where adversarial relationships exist with the opposing party.
sections.analysis:
You are required to disclose income annually pursuant to 
§87207(b) of the Government Code which provides in pertinent 
part: 
" 
(2) In the case of a business entity 
which provides legal or brokerage services, 
the name of every person who paid fees to 
the business entity if the filer's pro rata 
share of fees from such person was equal to 
or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000)." 


Chairman Jack Ni 
January 5, 1980 
Page Two 
This section was upheld in Hays v. Wood 25 C 3d 772, 160 
Cal. Rptr. 102, and a procedure was established to withhold 
the identity of a source of income. 
In any case, an income 
statement for 1980 is not yet due (see Government Code 
§87203) and it is unknown if you had the same income from 
MCPhail's in 1980 as you did in 1979. 
You are required to 
dis9ualify yourself from voting where a governmental 
dec1sion is to be made in which you have a financial interest 
(Government Code §87100). 
A financial interest is present "if it is reasonably foresee-
able that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect-on the public generally on 
••• any source of income ••• aggregating $250.00 or more in 
value ••• to the public official ••• " (Government Code §87103). 
This mea~s that if you have clients in the Third Street 
project area which you have not previously disclosed because 
the income to you was less than $1,000, you must now disclose 
any such clients who have paid you as little as $250 in the 
last 12 months. 
Such income may prove, upon analysis, to 
disquulify you from voting. 
The more than $1,000 income 
from McPhail's, if again received in 1980, appears to 
disqualify you from voting on this matter for three reasons: 
1. It exceeds $250. 
2. 
MCPhail's is directly impacted by the plan due to its 
location at Third and nCR Streets. 
3. 
Substantial enhancement to the McPhail property by the 
addition by the Redevelopment Agency of a parking lot 
lot next to the McPhail store. 
The Planning Department analysis of the financial effect will 
allow legal guidelines to be applied which will provide a 
basis to judge whether or not a material financial effect on 
your income is foreseeable.

Redevelopment Agency Executive Director 
Agency Secretary 
Messrs. Bernstein and Peterson (Hand Delivered)

Lee C. Rosenthal, Esq. 
Goldfarb & Owens Law Offices 
1333 Broadway, Suite 825 
Oakland, California 
94612 
January 6 8 198. 
Re: 
Legal Opinion Regarding Agency Chairman Jack Nixon -
Third Street Improvement Project -
Negative Declaration 
Dear Lee: 
Following the public hearing regarding the captioned matter, the Redevelopment 
Agency informally decided to postpone taking formal action pending the outcome 
of a legal opinion to be drafted which addresses the ability of Redevelopment 
Agency Chairman Jack Nixon to participate in the referenced matter, taking 
into consideration the fact that McPhail's (which has a retail outlet bor-
dering on Third Street) is a source of income for Mr. Nixon. 
Although, strictly speaking, Mr. Nixon's true sources of income are the 
principal agents, i.e., the large insurance companies, he has nonetheless 
listed those customers or clients from whom he receives more than a $1,000 
annually in income. 
McPhail's is one such source of income. 
The retail store 
in San Rafael on Third and C Streets is but one part of the entity which com-
prises McPhail's, a Marin County building supplier. 
(See enclosed description 
from 1 FPPC Opinions 199, December 4, 1975.) 
Because of a prior opinion rendered by your office in this matter (see enclosec 
letter) we feel it appropriate for you again to render an opinion concerning 
this complex and difficult area of law. 
If you require further information as a factual basis for your opinion, do not 
hesitate to contact either myself or Mr. Nixon at 472-0232. 
Thank you for your assistance.

sections.question_synthetic: Does the FPPC intend to intervene in pending litigation, and what are the disqualification requirements for public officials with financial interests in matters before them?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC will not intervene in the litigation and clarifies that administrative exhaustion is not required for injunctive relief. Disqualification is required for decisions with financial impact, but removal is not available under the Act, and the FPPC declines to advise on specific factual scenarios involving the adverse party.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and image quality degradation affecting readability. Multiple embedded documents (inter-departmental memos, correspondence) are included. Core advice letter sections are identifiable but text is partially corrupted. The main FPPC response letter is dated March 11, 1981, but surrounding documents create context about a San Rafael conflict of interest matter involving the Third Street Project Area.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Fair Political Practices Commission intends to intervene in litigation between the requester and Mr. Smith, and specific questions regarding disqualification requirements and conflict of interest determinations under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Commission does not intend to intervene in the litigation. Section 91003(a) does not mandate that a plaintiff file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunctive relief; such administrative exhaustion is required only when seeking damages or penalties under sections 91004, 91005, 91007. The Political Reform Act requires disqualification only in decisions where a public official has a financial interest (Section 87100), but does not provide for removal from office. The FPPC cannot provide advice on specific factual situations where adversarial relationships exist with the opposing party.

embedding.summary: This is a multi-part FPPC advice letter from March 1981 addressing a San Rafael city attorney's requests regarding intervention in litigation, disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act, and conflict of interest analysis for a redevelopment project. The FPPC declines intervention and advises that administrative exhaustion applies only to damage claims, while clarifying disqualification requirements and declining to advise on specific factual disputes.
